main_sections.title: The Pot Maker
main_sections.prose_summary: The story, written by Temsula Ao and set in a Naga village, follows young Sentila, who from early childhood is passionately drawn to the ancient craft of pot making practised by her mother Arenla and grandmother before her. However, Arenla, disillusioned by the physical hardship and meagre earnings of the craft — the clay must be dug from a riverbank sixteen kilometres away, carried uphill, pounded in bamboo cylinders for days, shaped with skilled hands and a spatula, sun-dried, and kiln-fired with painstaking care — wants her daughter to become a weaver instead, a profession she regards as cleaner, faster, and far more lucrative. Sentila secretly watches expert potters at work and is entranced by the rhythm of the spatula and the miracle of a shapeless lump becoming a vessel, but she keeps this fascination hidden at home after overhearing her mother's frank complaints to her father Mesoba. When the village community grows concerned that Arenla is refusing to pass on a hereditary skill, Mesoba is summoned by the village council, which reminds him that traditional crafts belong to the community, not to any individual, and that experts have a duty to teach anyone willing to learn. Mesoba speaks diplomatically and pledges that Sentila will be taught. Arenla then takes Sentila to the riverbank and instructs her in every stage — digging clay with a dao, soaking and pounding it, loading the kiln — but when it comes to shaping, Sentila's tension prevents the clay from yielding. A year of instruction passes with no real progress. The turning point arrives when Sentila, staying in the community girls' dormitory, is observed one evening by Onula, the wise middle-aged widow who supervises it. Onula watches the girl's clumsy, tense attempts and intervenes with quiet kindness, fashioning a perfect pot herself and then coaching Sentila to relax. Sentila creates her first beautiful pot, but Onula points out the mouth is wrong and directs her to watch her mother's hands more carefully at the critical moment. Armed with this specific instruction and a new confidence, Sentila observes Arenla intently at the next session and absorbs the technique of slackening the rhythm and adding an elongated dough strip for the rim. The climax arrives on a bright sunny day when Arenla, pleading a headache, asks Sentila to continue alone. Sentila, surprised and reluctant, begins — and finds herself making pot after pot with the speed and dexterity she had always admired in her mother's hands. She produces nearly a full batch, falling only one pot short of her mother's tally. When she walks to the house to join her mother for lunch, she finds Arenla lying lifeless on the threshold. As Arenla's body is carried from the house the next morning, Sentila runs after it, crying out that she had not wished it this way, that it had simply come to her. Only Onula, entering the work shed out of curiosity, understands: she discovers two indistinguishable rows of moist pots — one by Arenla, one by Sentila — standing side by side in perfect symmetry. Onula stands in awe before this profound revelation. The story ends with the spare, resonant sentence: 'A new pot maker was born.'

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Reflect and Respond + Reading for Meaning + Check Your Understanding + Critical Reflection

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I Look at the pictures given below and identify the vocations. Now, list at least five more vocations.
II Work in pairs. Discuss the following questions and share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: What is common among these pictures?
We refer to such skill-based work as v__ __ a __ __ o __ s.
Mention a few differences between handmade and machine-made products.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Check Your Understanding I (after Part I of the prose): Do you think pot making is easy? If yes, why? If no, why not?
Check Your Understanding II (after Part I): Would Sentila be able to fulfil her dream of becoming a pot maker? Explain.
Check Your Understanding III (after Part I): Do you think Mesoba and Arenla would support Sentila? Give a reason.
Check Your Understanding I (after Part II): Do you think Onula's support helped Sentila? If yes, why? If no, why not?
Check Your Understanding II (after Part II): Sentila observes her mother making pots. What does this tell us about her?
Critical Reflection I — Arrange the following events of the story in the correct sequence. Share your answer with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: The village council called Mesoba to know about Arenla's unwillingness to teach pottery to Sentila.
Arenla made a new batch of pots and asked Sentila to continue the work as she was unwell.
Sentila observed how other expert potters crafted beautiful pots.
Sentila was passionate about pottery but did not share it with her mother.
Onula guided Sentila in the art of pot making.
Sentila overheard her mother saying that pot making was a tiring job and that she earned very little from it.
Onula observed two rows of pots inside the work shed, which she felt was the work of two people.
Sentila learnt the art of pot making for a year from her mother, but was unsuccessful.
Sentila was able to make pots quickly and skillfully, just one less than her mother's.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Critical Reflection I — Read the extracts given below and answer the questions that follow. Extract 1: 'Pounding the stubborn clay inside bamboo cylinders to soften it, is also tedious. So many times I've dropped the mould out of sheer exhaustion and have had to start all over again. It takes months to bring out a batch of pots after so much labour. And the reward? A few rupees. But if Sentila learns weaving, she can make much more money besides providing enough cloth for the family. Weaving is not messy like pot making and can be done indoors in all seasons. Also, the time spent on weaving one shawl is much less and the return is handsome.'

question_bank.stem: Choose the correct reason for the given assertion. (A): The effort in making pots is far greater than the returns. Options: A. The process of pot making is quite tiresome and long, and one hardly earns much. B. The process of pot making is exhausting, takes a long time, yet gives a sense of satisfaction.
options: A. The process of pot making is quite tiresome and long, and one hardly earns much.
B. The process of pot making is exhausting, takes a long time, yet gives a sense of satisfaction.

question_bank.stem: Why does Arenla want Sentila to learn weaving?
State one advantage that weaving has over pot making, as per the extract.
Choose the sentence that uses the word 'handsome' in the same way as in the extract. A. My father is a handsome man. B. They will make a handsome profit selling this property.
options: A. My father is a handsome man.
B. They will make a handsome profit selling this property.

question_bank.stem: 'And the reward?' What is the author's purpose of using a question mark here?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Critical Reflection I — Extract 2: 'Onula saw her taking out some clay and the implements from her basket quietly. She watched Sentila's clumsy efforts to make a pot and noticed that Sentila was too tense. As a result, the clay seemed unable or unwilling to yield the right shape. When Sentila wearily let the misshapen lump fall flat on the ground, Onula went to her and said, "Don't worry, little one, I shall teach you how to make a perfect pot." Sentila watched in amazement as Onula fashioned a beautiful pot and asked her to try again.'

question_bank.stem: Complete the sentence with an appropriate reason. Onula feels Sentila's effort at making a pot is clumsy because ______________.

Choose the correct option to complete the following sentence. 'Don't worry, little one, I shall teach you how to make a perfect pot.' This shows that Onula was _____________. A. sincere and generous B. forgiving and thoughtful C. thoughtful and generous D. forgiving and sincere

Which among the following is the effect of a cause? A. As a result, the clay seemed unable or unwilling to yield the right shape. B. Onula saw her taking out some clay and the implements from her basket quietly.
options: A. As a result, the clay seemed unable or unwilling to yield the right shape.
B. Onula saw her taking out some clay and the implements from her basket quietly.

question_bank.stem: 'Onula fashioned a beautiful pot.' Here, the word 'fashioned' means ______________. (created/styled)
How might Sentila have felt when she saw 'the misshapen lump fall flat on the ground'?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Critical Reflection II — Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Describe the process of pot making followed by expert pot makers, as observed by Sentila.
What warning was given to Mesoba by the village council?
How did Sentila feel when she failed at pot making even after a year of training with her mother?
'Onula stood there for a long time as if trying to absorb a new phenomenon'. Explain.
'The tradition and history of the people did not belong to any individual,' what does this symbolise?
What is the significance of the concluding line of the story, 'A new pot maker was born'?
What is the role of perseverance in pursuing one's dreams? Elaborate with reference to Sentila.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I Read the highlighted words in the following sentences from the text. Now, classify the words/phrases given in the box as shown in the table below. One example for each category has been done for you. Words: dao, dough, pounding, bamboo, clay, kiln, spatula, basket, cylinders, rotating, bed of hay, shaping. Categories: Tools/Implements | Raw Materials | Process

II Notice the use of the following words in the text: pittance, rupees, reward, return. These words relate to livelihood and economic aspects. Work in pairs and find the meanings of the following words related to economy: bankrupt, credit, currency, debt, fiscal, inflation, investment, interest. Now, frame sentences using each word.
III.1 Complete the following sentences with suitable noun clauses.

question_bank.stem: The elders emphasised that ___________________.
Mesoba explained why ____________________.
Onula's promise was that ____________________.
Sentila observed her mother carefully when she was shaping the mouth of the pot, which ____________________.
The kiln, where ____________________, required careful attention to prevent over-or-under firing.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: III.2 Read the following sentences from the text. Underline the main clause and circle the subordinate clause.

question_bank.stem: Arenla took Sentila to the riverbank where the grey and red clay was found.
She started on the next one, and like a sprinter who had suddenly found momentum...
… skills such as pot making, which not only catered to the needs of the people...

tasks_verbatim.task_text: III.3 Complete the following sentences with suitable relative clauses.

question_bank.stem: Sentila, whose ______________, practised the craft diligently.
The village council, where ____________, sought an explanation for Arenla's reluctance.
The potter's hands, which _____________, shaped the clay into beautiful creations.
Arenla, her mother, wanted her to learn weaving, which ______________________.
Mesoba went home and discussed the matter with Arenla, who _____________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: III.4(i) Find out some more determiners from the text.
III.4(ii) Fill in the blanks with suitable determiners. The first two blanks have been done for you.

question_bank.stem: A. The florist arranged five bouquets for her clients, that were displayed in ____________ elegant floral shop.
B. The carpenter crafted ____________ unique tables, and _____________________ became the centrepiece in ____________________ furniture collection.
C. ____________ of ____________ apprentices in ____________ culinary class demonstrated ____________ knife skills during the intense cooking session.
D. ____________ of ____________ sculptures were displayed at _______________ art exhibition, showcasing ___________ diverse artistic skills.

listening.transcript_text: Task I — A man speaks about stone statues: Stone statues are made by carving or assembling stone to form a visually interesting three-dimensional shape. Since early civilisation, stone has been used in architectural sculpture on building exteriors; today it is mostly used for sculptures. Evidence of stonework spans early societies in India, Egypt, Iran, and Greece, with India holding some of the most fascinating and mesmerising examples of stone monuments across the country. Task II — The man describes steps in making a stone sculpture: The process begins with the sculptor choosing a stone according to the required weight and dimensions. The sculptor then carves the stone, beginning by removing large unwanted portions. Next, the sculptor works to bring out the imagined shape from the block. Different tools are used throughout. The sculptor removes material, sketches on the block, and develops the work along the way — creating the fully dimensional form directly in the stone. The creation is then detached from the block of stone as the final statue, which the sculptor refines with finishing touches.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I You will listen to a man speak about stone statues. As you listen, complete the given paragraph by filling in the blanks with the exact words you listen to. 'A statue is carved to create a shape that is 1. _________. Among the many things stone is used for making stone 2. _____________ is one of them. India has some of the most 3. _____________ stone sculptures, as is obvious from its many stone monuments across the country.'

question_bank.stem: Blank 1 (shape that is ___)
Blank 2 (making stone ___)
Blank 3 (most ___ stone sculptures)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: II You will now listen to the man speaking about some steps involved in making stone statues. As you listen, select the six correct steps out of the nine given.

question_bank.stem: 1. carve to remove large unwanted portions of the stone
2. set up the different tools
3. measure the weight and dimensions of the statue
4. leave the statue in water to firm up overnight
5. refine the creation within the stone
6. choose the stone
7. begin carving from the centre
8. detach the creation from the stone as the final statue
9. work to bring out the imagined shape

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Work in pairs and choose two characters from the story — Sentila and one other character [Arenla, Mesoba, Onula, or a village elder]. Prepare to speak from the chosen character's perspective based on information from the story and the understanding of the intentions of the characters. Prepare a role-play between Sentila and the chosen character. The conversation between the characters should cover: Sentila's desire to learn pot making; the challenges she faces; the advice or perspective the other character offers. Students can use direct quotes from the story and creatively expand on the characters' thoughts and feelings. Sentence prompts: 'I feel/felt __________ because...', 'I wish/wished __________ because...', 'When you said/did __________, it made me feel/think __________ because...'

Reflective Writing – Identifying Skills and Passion. Follow the steps given below to create a write-up about your skills and passions. Step 1: Introduction — Reflect on your passions and the skills you currently possess or are developing. Consider why these activities or interests are meaningful and enjoyable to you. Step 2: Describing skills — Describe specific activities or practices you engage in to nurture your skills. Step 3: Passion into profession — Identify which of these skills you believe have the potential to turn your passion into a profession. Explain why you think these skills are crucial or advantageous in your chosen field. Step 4: Examples and reflection — Provide examples or anecdotes that illustrate how your skills and passions complement each other. Reflect on how these experiences have shaped your career aspirations and personal growth. Step 5: Conclusion — Summarise your reflections. Discuss any insights gained about yourself, your skills, and your career ambitions through this exercise.

question_bank.stem: Step 1: Introduction
Step 2: Describing skills
Step 3: Passion into profession
Step 4: Examples and reflection
Step 5: Conclusion

tasks_verbatim.task_text: I Pottery is practised in many parts of our country and each region has its unique style. Find out about the different styles of indigenous pottery of your region and other regions of our country. Some notable styles include: Khurja pottery from Uttar Pradesh, Blue pottery from Jaipur, Terracotta from West Bengal, Andretta pottery from Himachal Pradesh, Karigari pottery from Tamil Nadu, Longpi Black pottery from Manipur. 1. Match the pictures with the pottery it represents.

question_bank.stem: Khurja pottery from Uttar Pradesh
Blue pottery from Jaipur
Terracotta from West Bengal
Andretta pottery from Himachal Pradesh
Karigari pottery from Tamil Nadu
Longpi Black pottery from Manipur

tasks_verbatim.task_text: II You must have read stories in English and in your own language about the value of handicrafts and art. Read the story 'Quality' by John Galsworthy. Write its review and make a presentation highlighting the decline of traditional crafts and skills vs. industrialisation and mass-produced goods. [Story provided in the textbook, pp.52–56.]
